revision.text:
{{Infobox ancient site | name = Teppōyama Kofun | native_name = 鉄砲山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Teppo-yama Kofun (Gyoda), zenkei.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Teppōyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = Location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Teppōyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|7|25.73|N|139|28|41.56|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Gyōda, Saitama]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kantō region]] | type = [[Kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = 6th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Teppōyama Kofun'''|鉄砲山古墳||}} is a ''[[kofun]]'' [[burial mound]] located in the city of [[Gyōda, Saitama|Gyōda]], [[Saitama Prefecture]], in the [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a National Historic Site in 1938 and re-designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2020 as part of the [[Sakitama Kofun Cluster]].<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/603 |title=埼玉古墳群|trans-title=Saikitama Kofun Cluster|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Teppōyama Kofun has a total length (including moat) of 163 meters, and is thus the third largest tumulus in the Saitama Kofun Cluster. It is a {{nihongo|''[[zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It has the same proportions as the [[Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun]], but shrunk by 80%. [[Archaeological excavation]]s have been conducted in 1967, 1983, 2008 and 2010 to 2011. Artifacts uncovered include cylindrical and "morning glory-shaped" ''[[haniwa]]'', [[Haji ware]], and [[Sue ware]] pottery, from which the tumulus is estimated to have been built in the latter half of the 6th century AD.
The ''kofun'' is highly unusual in that it has a triple moat, which is a very rare feature; however, traces of the outermost moat have survived only on the western side of the tumulus, and it is not certain if it once completed encircled the mound. Similar triple-moated ''kofun'' include The Mitsuke Kofun in [[Kurume, Fukuoka]], Tsukioka Kofun in [[Ukiha, Fukuoka]] and the Otsumi Gongenzuka Kofun in [[Sammu, Chiba]].
Another unusual feature is a protrusion extending from the western side of the tumulus, near the joint between the rectangular and circular portions. This is a feature which also appears in the [[Inariyama Kofun]], Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun, and {{ill|Shogunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳}} in the Sakitama Kofun Group, but is rarely found in other locations. Called a "Tsukuridashi", it was apparently a platform used for rituals.
From the results of [[ground-penetrating radar]] surveys and excavation on the posterior circular portion of the tumulus, it is known that there is a horizontal-type stone [[burial chamber]] that is orientated to the south. However, no excavation of the burial chamber itself has been conducted, and it is unknown whether or not it has been robbed in antiquity. The entrance to the burial chamber was thickly covered with clay. A Sue ware pot with a perforated bottom has been excavated from the vestibule.
The name "Teppōyama" comes from the fact that tumulus was used as a gunnery training center of [[Oshi Domain]] during the [[Edo period]]. The training ground was located near the northern constriction, and the mound was modified to form the backstop behind targets used in rifle practice with an embankment constructed to shelter the person near the targets who confirmed whether or not the shooter's aim was correct. A total of 154 bullets in18 varieties have also been found at the site.
;Overall length: 109 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 55 meter diameter x 9.0 meter high ;Anterior rectangular portion: 69 meters wide x 10.1 meters high
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Saitama)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons category-inline|Teppoyama Kofun}} *[https://www.city.gyoda.lg.jp/15/04/12/map/tetu.html Gyōda city home page] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://sakitama-muse.spec.ed.jp/埼玉古墳群紹介/鉄砲山古墳 Museum of the Sakitama Ancient Burial Mounds] {{in lang|ja}} [[Category:History of Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Gyōda]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Sakitama Kofun Cluster]]

The tumulus was designated a National Historic Site in 1938 and re-designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2020 as part of the [[Sakitama Kofun Cluster]].<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/603 |title=埼玉古墳群|trans-title=Saikitama Kofun Cluster|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Teppōyama Kofun has a total length (including moat) of 163 meters, and is thus the third largest tumulus in the Saitama Kofun Cluster. It is a {{nihongo|''[[zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It has the same proportions as the [[Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun]], but shrunk by 80%. [[Archaeological excavation]]s have been conducted in 1967, 1983, 2008 and 2010 to 2011. Artifacts uncovered include cylindrical and "morning glory-shaped" ''[[haniwa]]'', [[Haji ware]], and [[Sue ware]] pottery, from which the tumulus is estimated to have been built in the latter half of the 6th century AD.
The ''kofun'' is highly unusual in that it has a triple moat, which is a very rare feature; however, traces of the outermost moat have survived only on the western side of the tumulus, and it is not certain if it once completed encircled the mound. Similar triple-moated ''kofun'' include The Mitsuke Kofun in [[Kurume, Fukuoka]], Tsukioka Kofun in [[Ukiha, Fukuoka]] and the Otsumi Gongenzuka Kofun in [[Sammu, Chiba]].
Another unusual feature is a protrusion extending from the western side of the tumulus, near the joint between the rectangular and circular portions. This is a feature which also appears in the [[Inariyama Kofun]], Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun, and {{ill|Shogunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳}} in the Sakitama Kofun Group, but is rarely found in other locations. Called a "Tsukuridashi", it was apparently a platform used for rituals.
From the results of [[ground-penetrating radar]] surveys and excavation on the posterior circular portion of the tumulus, it is known that there is a horizontal-type stone [[burial chamber]] that is orientated to the south. However, no excavation of the burial chamber itself has been conducted, and it is unknown whether or not it has been robbed in antiquity. The entrance to the burial chamber was thickly covered with clay. A Sue ware pot with a perforated bottom has been excavated from the vestibule.
The name "Teppōyama" comes from the fact that tumulus was used as a gunnery training center of [[Oshi Domain]] during the [[Edo period]]. The training ground was located near the northern constriction, and the mound was modified to form the backstop behind targets used in rifle practice with an embankment constructed to shelter the person near the targets who confirmed whether or not the shooter's aim was correct. A total of 154 bullets in18 varieties have also been found at the site.
;Overall length: 109 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 55 meter diameter x 9.0 meter high ;Anterior rectangular portion: 69 meters wide x 10.1 meters high

{{Infobox ancient site | name = Teppōyama Kofun | native_name = 鉄砲山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Teppo-yama Kofun (Gyoda), zenkei.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Teppōyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Teppōyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|7|25.73|N|139|28|41.56|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Gyōda, Saitama]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kantō region]] | type = [[Kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = 6th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Teppōyama Kofun'''|鉄砲山古墳||}} is a ''[[kofun]]'' [[burial mound]] located in the city of [[Gyōda, Saitama|Gyōda]], [[Saitama Prefecture]], in the [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a National Historic Site in 1938 and re-designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2020 as part of the [[Sakitama Kofun Cluster]].<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/603 |title=埼玉古墳群|trans-title=Saikitama Kofun Cluster|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Teppōyama Kofun has a total length (including moat) of 163 meters, and is thus the third largest tumulus in the Saitama Kofun Cluster. It is a {{nihongo|''[[zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It has the same proportions as the [[Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun]], but shrunk by 80%. [[Archaeological excavation]]s have been conducted in 1967, 1983, 2008 and 2010 to 2011. Artifacts uncovered include cylindrical and "morning glory-shaped" ''[[haniwa]]'', [[Haji ware]], and [[Sue ware]] pottery, from which the tumulus is estimated to have been built in the latter half of the 6th century AD.
The ''kofun'' is highly unusual in that it has a triple moat, which is a very rare feature; however, traces of the outermost moat have survived only on the western side of the tumulus, and it is not certain if it once completed encircled the mound. Similar triple-moated ''kofun'' include The Mitsuke Kofun in [[Kurume, Fukuoka]], Tsukioka Kofun in [[Ukiha, Fukuoka]] and the Otsumi Gongenzuka Kofun in [[Sammu, Chiba]].
Another unusual feature is a protrusion extending from the western side of the tumulus, near the joint between the rectangular and circular portions. This is a feature which also appears in the [[Inariyama Kofun]], Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun, and {{ill|Shogunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳}} in the Sakitama Kofun Group, but is rarely found in other locations. Called a "Tsukuridashi", it was apparently a platform used for rituals.
From the results of [[ground-penetrating radar]] surveys and excavation on the posterior circular portion of the tumulus, it is known that there is a horizontal-type stone [[burial chamber]] that is orientated to the south. However, no excavation of the burial chamber itself has been conducted, and it is unknown whether or not it has been robbed in antiquity. The entrance to the burial chamber was thickly covered with clay. A Sue ware pot with a perforated bottom has been excavated from the vestibule.
The name "Teppōyama" comes from the fact that tumulus was used as a gunnery training center of [[Oshi Domain]] during the [[Edo period]]. The training ground was located near the northern constriction, and the mound was modified to form the backstop behind targets used in rifle practice with an embankment constructed to shelter the person near the targets who confirmed whether or not the shooter's aim was correct. A total of 154 bullets in18 varieties have also been found at the site.
;Overall length: 109 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 55 meter diameter x 9.0 meter high ;Anterior rectangular portion: 69 meters wide x 10.1 meters high

The tumulus was designated a National Historic Site in 1938 and re-designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2020 as part of the [[Sakitama Kofun Cluster]].<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/603 |title=埼玉古墳群|trans-title=Saikitama Kofun Cluster|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Teppōyama Kofun has a total length (including moat) of 163 meters, and is thus the third largest tumulus in the Saitama Kofun Cluster. It is a {{nihongo|''[[zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It has the same proportions as the [[Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun]], but shrunk by 80%. [[Archaeological excavation]]s have been conducted in 1967, 1983, 2008 and 2010 to 2011. Artifacts uncovered include cylindrical and "morning glory-shaped" ''[[haniwa]]'', [[Haji ware]], and [[Sue ware]] pottery, from which the tumulus is estimated to have been built in the latter half of the 6th century AD.
The ''kofun'' is highly unusual in that it has a triple moat, which is a very rare feature; however, traces of the outermost moat have survived only on the western side of the tumulus, and it is not certain if it once completed encircled the mound. Similar triple-moated ''kofun'' include The Mitsuke Kofun in [[Kurume, Fukuoka]], Tsukioka Kofun in [[Ukiha, Fukuoka]] and the Otsumi Gongenzuka Kofun in [[Sammu, Chiba]].
Another unusual feature is a protrusion extending from the western side of the tumulus, near the joint between the rectangular and circular portions. This is a feature which also appears in the [[Inariyama Kofun]], Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun, and {{ill|Shogunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳}} in the Sakitama Kofun Group, but is rarely found in other locations. Called a "Tsukuridashi", it was apparently a platform used for rituals.
From the results of [[ground-penetrating radar]] surveys and excavation on the posterior circular portion of the tumulus, it is known that there is a horizontal-type stone [[burial chamber]] that is orientated to the south. However, no excavation of the burial chamber itself has been conducted, and it is unknown whether or not it has been robbed in antiquity. The entrance to the burial chamber was thickly covered with clay. A Sue ware pot with a perforated bottom has been excavated from the vestibule.
The name "Teppōyama" comes from the fact that tumulus was used as a gunnery training center of [[Oshi Domain]] during the [[Edo period]]. The training ground was located near the northern constriction, and the mound was modified to form the backstop behind targets used in rifle practice with an embankment constructed to shelter the person near the targets who confirmed whether or not the shooter's aim was correct. A total of 154 bullets in18 varieties have also been found at the site.
;Overall length: 109 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 55 meter diameter x 9.0 meter high ;Anterior rectangular portion: 69 meters wide x 10.1 meters high
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Saitama)]]

The tumulus was designated a National Historic Site in 1938 and re-designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2020 as part of the [[Sakitama Kofun Cluster]].<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/603 |title=埼玉古墳群|trans-title=Saikitama Kofun Cluster|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Teppōyama Kofun has a total length (including moat) of 163 meters, and is thus the third largest tumulus in the Saitama Kofun Cluster. It is a {{nihongo|''[[zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It has the same proportions as the [[Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun]], but shrunk by 80%. [[Archaeological excavation]]s have been conducted in 1967, 1983, 2008 and 2010 to 2011. Artifacts uncovered include cylindrical and "morning glory-shaped" ''[[haniwa]]'', [[Haji ware]], and [[Sue ware]] pottery, from which the tumulus is estimated to have been built in the latter half of the 6th century AD.
The ''kofun'' is highly unusual in that it has a triple moat, which is a very rare feature; however, traces of the outermost moat have survived only on the western side of the tumulus, and it is not certain if it once completed encircled the mound. Similar triple-moated ''kofun'' include The Mitsuke Kofun in [[Kurume, Fukuoka]], Tsukioka Kofun in [[Ukiha, Fukuoka]] and the Otsumi Gongenzuka Kofun in [[Sammu, Chiba]].
Another unusual feature is a protrusion extending from the western side of the tumulus, near the joint between the rectangular and circular portions. This is a feature which also appears in the [[Inariyama Kofun]], Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun, and {{ill|Shogunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳}} in the Sakitama Kofun Group, but is rarely found in other locations. Called a "Tsukuridashi", it was apparently a platform used for rituals.
From the results of [[ground-penetrating radar]] surveys and excavation on the posterior circular portion of the tumulus, it is known that there is a horizontal-type stone [[burial chamber]] that is orientated to the south. However, no excavation of the burial chamber itself has been conducted, and it is unknown whether or not it has been robbed in antiquity. The entrance to the burial chamber was thickly covered with clay. A Sue ware pot with a perforated bottom has been excavated from the vestibule.
The name "Teppōyama" comes from the fact that tumulus was used as a gunnery training center of [[Oshi Domain]] during the [[Edo period]]. The training ground was located near the northern constriction, and the mound was modified to form the backstop behind targets used in rifle practice with an embankment constructed to shelter the person near the targets who confirmed whether or not the shooter's aim was correct. A total of 154 bullets in18 varieties have also been found at the site.
;Overall length: 109 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 55 meter diameter x 9.0 meter high ;Anterior rectangular portion: 69 meters wide x 10.1 meters high
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Saitama)]]

{{Infobox ancient site | name = Teppōyama Kofun | native_name = 鉄砲山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Teppo-yama Kofun (Gyoda), zenkei.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Teppōyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Teppōyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|7|25.73|N|139|28|41.56|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Gyōda, Saitama]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kantō region]] | type = [[Kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = 6th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Teppōyama Kofun'''|鉄砲山古墳||}} is a ''[[kofun]]'' [[burial mound]] located in the city of [[Gyōda, Saitama|Gyōda]], [[Saitama Prefecture]], in the [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a National Historic Site in 1938 and re-designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2020 as part of the [[Sakitama Kofun Cluster]].<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/603 |title=埼玉古墳群|trans-title=Saikitama Kofun Cluster|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Teppōyama Kofun has a total length (including moat) of 163 meters, and is thus the third largest tumulus in the Saitama Kofun Cluster. It is a {{nihongo|''[[zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It has the same proportions as the [[Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun]], but shrunk by 80%. [[Archaeological excavation]]s have been conducted in 1967, 1983, 2008 and 2010 to 2011. Artifacts uncovered include cylindrical and "morning glory-shaped" ''[[haniwa]]'', [[Haji ware]], and [[Sue ware]] pottery, from which the tumulus is estimated to have been built in the latter half of the 6th century AD.
The ''kofun'' is highly unusual in that it has a triple moat, which is a very rare feature; however, traces of the outermost moat have survived only on the western side of the tumulus, and it is not certain if it once completed encircled the mound. Similar triple-moated ''kofun'' include The Mitsuke Kofun in [[Kurume, Fukuoka]], Tsukioka Kofun in [[Ukiha, Fukuoka]] and the Otsumi Gongenzuka Kofun in [[Sammu, Chiba]].
Another unusual feature is a protrusion extending from the western side of the tumulus, near the joint between the rectangular and circular portions. This is a feature which also appears in the [[Inariyama Kofun]], Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun, and {{ill|Shogunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳}} in the Sakitama Kofun Group, but is rarely found in other locations. Called a "Tsukuridashi", it was apparently a platform used for rituals.
From the results of [[ground-penetrating radar]] surveys and excavation on the posterior circular portion of the tumulus, it is known that there is a horizontal-type stone [[burial chamber]] that is orientated to the south. However, no excavation of the burial chamber itself has been conducted, and it is unknown whether or not it has been robbed in antiquity. The entrance to the burial chamber was thickly covered with clay. A Sue ware pot with a perforated bottom has been excavated from the vestibule.
The name "Teppōyama" comes from the fact that tumulus was used as a gunnery training center of [[Oshi Domain]] during the [[Edo period]]. The training ground was located near the northern constriction, and the mound was modified to form the backstop behind targets used in rifle practice with an embankment constructed to shelter the person near the targets who confirmed whether or not the shooter's aim was correct. A total of 154 bullets in18 varieties have also been found at the site.
;Overall length: 109 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 55 meter diameter x 9.0 meter high ;Anterior rectangular portion: 69 meters wide x 10.1 meters high
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Saitama)]]
==References== {{reflist}}

{{Infobox ancient site | name = Teppōyama Kofun | native_name = 鉄砲山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Teppo-yama Kofun (Gyoda), zenkei.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Teppōyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Teppōyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|7|25.73|N|139|28|41.56|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Gyōda, Saitama]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kantō region]] | type = [[Kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = 6th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Teppōyama Kofun'''|鉄砲山古墳||}} is a ''[[kofun]]'' [[burial mound]] located in the city of [[Gyōda, Saitama|Gyōda]], [[Saitama Prefecture]], in the [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a National Historic Site in 1938 and re-designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2020 as part of the [[Sakitama Kofun Cluster]].<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/603 |title=埼玉古墳群|trans-title=Saikitama Kofun Cluster|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Teppōyama Kofun has a total length (including moat) of 163 meters, and is thus the third largest tumulus in the Saitama Kofun Cluster. It is a {{nihongo|''[[zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It has the same proportions as the [[Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun]], but shrunk by 80%. [[Archaeological excavation]]s have been conducted in 1967, 1983, 2008 and 2010 to 2011. Artifacts uncovered include cylindrical and "morning glory-shaped" ''[[haniwa]]'', [[Haji ware]], and [[Sue ware]] pottery, from which the tumulus is estimated to have been built in the latter half of the 6th century AD.
The ''kofun'' is highly unusual in that it has a triple moat, which is a very rare feature; however, traces of the outermost moat have survived only on the western side of the tumulus, and it is not certain if it once completed encircled the mound. Similar triple-moated ''kofun'' include The Mitsuke Kofun in [[Kurume, Fukuoka]], Tsukioka Kofun in [[Ukiha, Fukuoka]] and the Otsumi Gongenzuka Kofun in [[Sammu, Chiba]].
Another unusual feature is a protrusion extending from the western side of the tumulus, near the joint between the rectangular and circular portions. This is a feature which also appears in the [[Inariyama Kofun]], Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun, and {{ill|Shogunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳}} in the Sakitama Kofun Group, but is rarely found in other locations. Called a "Tsukuridashi", it was apparently a platform used for rituals.
From the results of [[ground-penetrating radar]] surveys and excavation on the posterior circular portion of the tumulus, it is known that there is a horizontal-type stone [[burial chamber]] that is orientated to the south. However, no excavation of the burial chamber itself has been conducted, and it is unknown whether or not it has been robbed in antiquity. The entrance to the burial chamber was thickly covered with clay. A Sue ware pot with a perforated bottom has been excavated from the vestibule.
The name "Teppōyama" comes from the fact that tumulus was used as a gunnery training center of [[Oshi Domain]] during the [[Edo period]]. The training ground was located near the northern constriction, and the mound was modified to form the backstop behind targets used in rifle practice with an embankment constructed to shelter the person near the targets who confirmed whether or not the shooter's aim was correct. A total of 154 bullets in18 varieties have also been found at the site.
;Overall length: 109 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 55 meter diameter x 9.0 meter high ;Anterior rectangular portion: 69 meters wide x 10.1 meters high
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Saitama)]]
==References== {{reflist}}

It is a {{nihongo|''[[zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It has the same proportions as the [[Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun]], but shrunk by 80%. [[Archaeological excavation]]s have been conducted in 1967, 1983, 2008 and 2010 to 2011. Artifacts uncovered include cylindrical and "morning glory-shaped" ''[[haniwa]]'', [[Haji ware]], and [[Sue ware]] pottery, from which the tumulus is estimated to have been built in the latter half of the 6th century AD.
The ''kofun'' is highly unusual in that it has a triple moat, which is a very rare feature; however, traces of the outermost moat have survived only on the western side of the tumulus, and it is not certain if it once completed encircled the mound. Similar triple-moated ''kofun'' include The Mitsuke Kofun in [[Kurume, Fukuoka]], Tsukioka Kofun in [[Ukiha, Fukuoka]] and the Otsumi Gongenzuka Kofun in [[Sammu, Chiba]].
Another unusual feature is a protrusion extending from the western side of the tumulus, near the joint between the rectangular and circular portions. This is a feature which also appears in the [[Inariyama Kofun]], Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun, and {{ill|Shogunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳}} in the Sakitama Kofun Group, but is rarely found in other locations. Called a "Tsukuridashi", it was apparently a platform used for rituals.
From the results of [[ground-penetrating radar]] surveys and excavation on the posterior circular portion of the tumulus, it is known that there is a horizontal-type stone [[burial chamber]] that is orientated to the south. However, no excavation of the burial chamber itself has been conducted, and it is unknown whether or not it has been robbed in antiquity. The entrance to the burial chamber was thickly covered with clay. A Sue ware pot with a perforated bottom has been excavated from the vestibule.
The name "Teppōyama" comes from the fact that tumulus was used as a gunnery training center of [[Oshi Domain]] during the [[Edo period]]. The training ground was located near the northern constriction, and the mound was modified to form the backstop behind targets used in rifle practice with an embankment constructed to shelter the person near the targets who confirmed whether or not the shooter's aim was correct. A total of 154 bullets in18 varieties have also been found at the site.

This is a feature which also appears in the [[Inariyama Kofun]], Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun, and {{ill|Shogunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳}} in the Sakitama Kofun Group, but is rarely found in other locations. Called a "Tsukuridashi", it was apparently a platform used for rituals.
From the results of [[ground-penetrating radar]] surveys and excavation on the posterior circular portion of the tumulus, it is known that there is a horizontal-type stone [[burial chamber]] that is orientated to the south. However, no excavation of the burial chamber itself has been conducted, and it is unknown whether or not it has been robbed in antiquity. The entrance to the burial chamber was thickly covered with clay. A Sue pottery pot with a perforated bottom has been excavated from the vestibule.
The name "Teppōyama" comes from the fact that tumulus was used as a gunnery training center of [[Oshi Domain]] during the [[Edo period]]. The training ground was located near the northern constriction, and the mound was modified to form the backstop behind targets used in rifle practice with an embankment constructed to shelter the person near the targets who confirmed whether or not the shooter's aim was correct. A total of 154 bullets in18 varieties have also been found at the site.

{{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] | name = Teppōyama Kofun | native_name = 鉄砲山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Teppo-yama Kofun (Gyoda), zenkei.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Teppōyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Teppōyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|7|25.73|N|139|28|41.56|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Gyōda, Saitama]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kantō region]] | type = [[Kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = 6th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Teppōyama Kofun'''|鉄砲山古墳||}} is a ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun]] located in the city of [[Gyōda, Saitama|Gyōda]], [[Saitama Prefecture]], in the [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a National Historic Site in 1938 and re-designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2020 as part of the [[Sakitama Kofun Cluster]].<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/603 |title=埼玉古墳群|trans-title=Saikitama Kofun Cluster|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Teppōyama Kofun has a total length (including moat) of 163 meters, and is thus the third largest tumulus in the Saitama Kofun Cluster. It is a {{nihongo|''[[zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It has the same proportions as the [[Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun]], but shrunk by 80%. [[Archaeological excavation]]s have been conducted in 1967, 1983, 2008 and 2010 to 2011. Artifacts uncovered include cylindrical and "morning glory-shaped" ''[[haniwa]]'', [[Haji pottery]], and [[Sue pottery]] pottery, from which the tumulus is estimated to have been built in the latter half of the 6th century AD.
The ''kofun'' is highly unusual in that it has a triple moat, which is a very rare feature; however, traces of the outermost moat have survived only on the western side of the tumulus, and it is not certain if it once completed encircled the mound. Similar triple-moated ''kofun'' include The Mitsuke Kofun in [[Kurume, Fukuoka]], Tsukioka Kofun in [[Ukiha, Fukuoka]] and the Otsumi Gongenzuka Kofun in [[Sammu, Chiba]].
Another unusual feature is a protrusion extending from the western side of the tumulus, near the joint between the rectangular and circular portions. This is a feature which also appears in the [[Inariyama Kofun]], Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun, and {{ill|Shogunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳}} in the Sakitama Kofun Group, but is rarely found in other locations. Called a "Tsukuridashi", it was apparently a platform used for rituals.
From the results of [[ground-penetrating radar]] surveys and excavation on the posterior circular portion of the tumulus, it is known that there is a horizontal-type stone [[burial chamber]] that is orientated to the south. However, no excavation of the burial chamber itself has been conducted, and it is unknown whether or not it has been robbed in antiquity. The entrance to the burial chamber was thickly covered with clay. A Sue pottery pot with a perforated bottom has been excavated from the vestibule.
The name "Teppōyama" comes from the fact that tumulus was used as a gunnery training center of [[Oshi Domain]] during the [[Edo period]]. The training ground was located near the northern constriction, and the mound was modified to form the backstop behind targets used in rifle practice with an embankment constructed to shelter the person near the targets who confirmed whether or not the shooter's aim was correct. A total of 154 bullets in18 varieties have also been found at the site.
;Overall length: 109 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 55 meter diameter x 9.0 meter high ;Anterior rectangular portion: 69 meters wide x 10.1 meters high
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Saitama)]]

[[Category:kk]] [[Category:Teppōyama Kofun]][[en:Teppōyama Kofun]] [[Category:completion tier 5]][[sn:Teppōyama Kofun]][[sn:Teppōyama Kofun]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] {{Kofun navbar}} [[Category:New]]

{{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] | name = Teppōyama Kofun | native_name = 鉄砲山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Teppo-yama Kofun (Gyoda), zenkei.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Teppōyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Teppōyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|7|25.73|N|139|28|41.56|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Gyōda, Saitama]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kantō region]] | type = [[Kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = 6th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes | Historic Site = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Teppōyama Kofun'''|鉄砲山古墳||}} is a ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun]] located in the city of [[Gyōda, Saitama|Gyōda]], [[Saitama Prefecture]], in the [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a National Historic Site in 1938 and re-designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2020 as part of the [[Sakitama Kofun Cluster]].<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/603 |title=埼玉古墳群|trans-title=Saikitama Kofun Cluster|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Teppōyama Kofun has a total length (including moat) of 163 meters, and is thus the third largest tumulus in the Saitama Kofun Cluster. It is a {{nihongo|''[[zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It has the same proportions as the [[Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun]], but shrunk by 80%. [[Archaeological excavation]]s have been conducted in 1967, 1983, 2008 and 2010 to 2011. Artifacts uncovered include cylindrical and "morning glory-shaped" ''[[haniwa]]'', [[Haji pottery]], and [[Sue pottery]] pottery, from which the tumulus is estimated to have been built in the latter half of the 6th century AD.
The ''kofun'' is highly unusual in that it has a triple moat, which is a very rare feature; however, traces of the outermost moat have survived only on the western side of the tumulus, and it is not certain if it once completed encircled the mound. Similar triple-moated ''kofun'' include The Mitsuke Kofun in [[Kurume, Fukuoka]], Tsukioka Kofun in [[Ukiha, Fukuoka]] and the Otsumi Gongenzuka Kofun in [[Sammu, Chiba]].
Another unusual feature is a protrusion extending from the western side of the tumulus, near the joint between the rectangular and circular portions. This is a feature which also appears in the [[Inariyama Kofun]], Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun, and {{ill|Shogunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳}} in the Sakitama Kofun Group, but is rarely found in other locations. Called a "Tsukuridashi", it was apparently a platform used for rituals.
From the results of [[ground-penetrating radar]] surveys and excavation on the posterior circular portion of the tumulus, it is known that there is a horizontal-type stone [[burial chamber]] that is orientated to the south. However, no excavation of the burial chamber itself has been conducted, and it is unknown whether or not it has been robbed in antiquity. The entrance to the burial chamber was thickly covered with clay. A Sue pottery pot with a perforated bottom has been excavated from the vestibule.
The name "Teppōyama" comes from the fact that tumulus was used as a gunnery training center of [[Oshi Domain]] during the [[Edo period]]. The training ground was located near the northern constriction, and the mound was modified to form the backstop behind targets used in rifle practice with an embankment constructed to shelter the person near the targets who confirmed whether or not the shooter's aim was correct. A total of 154 bullets in18 varieties have also been found at the site.
;Overall length: 109 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 55 meter diameter x 9.0 meter high ;Anterior rectangular portion: 69 meters wide x 10.1 meters high
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Saitama)]]
==References== {{reflist}}

Similar triple-moated ''kofun'' include The Mitsuke Kofun in [[Kurume, Fukuoka]], Tsukioka Kofun in [[Ukiha, Fukuoka]] and the Otsumi Gongenzuka Kofun in [[Sammu, Chiba]].
Another unusual feature is a protrusion extending from the western side of the tumulus, near the joint between the rectangular and circular portions. This is a feature which also appears in the [[Inariyama Kofun]], Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun, and {{ill|Shogunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳|12=draft}} in the Sakitama Kofun Group, but is rarely found in other locations. Called a "Tsukuridashi", it was apparently a platform used for rituals.
From the results of [[ground-penetrating radar]] surveys and excavation on the posterior circular portion of the tumulus, it is known that there is a horizontal-type stone [[burial chamber]] that is orientated to the south. However, no excavation of the burial chamber itself has been conducted, and it is unknown whether or not it has been robbed in antiquity. The entrance to the burial chamber was thickly covered with clay. A Sue pottery pot with a perforated bottom has been excavated from the vestibule.
The name "Teppōyama" comes from the fact that tumulus was used as a gunnery training center of [[Oshi Domain]] during the [[Edo period]]. The training ground was located near the northern constriction, and the mound was modified to form the backstop behind targets used in rifle practice with an embankment constructed to shelter the person near the targets who confirmed whether or not the shooter's aim was correct. A total of 154 bullets in18 varieties have also been found at the site.
;Overall length: 109 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 55 meter diameter x 9.0 meter high ;Anterior rectangular portion: 69 meters wide x 10.1 meters high

From the results of [[ground-penetrating radar]] surveys and excavation on the posterior circular portion of the tumulus, it is known that there is a horizontal-type stone [[burial chamber]] that is orientated to the south. However, no excavation of the burial chamber itself has been conducted, and it is unknown whether or not it has been robbed in antiquity. The entrance to the burial chamber was thickly covered with clay. A Sue pottery pot with a perforated bottom has been excavated from the vestibule.
The name "Teppōyama" comes from the fact that tumulus was used as a gunnery training center of [[Oshi Domain]] during the [[Edo period]]. The training ground was located near the northern constriction, and the mound was modified to form the backstop behind targets used in rifle practice with an embankment constructed to shelter the person near the targets who confirmed whether or not the shooter's aim was correct. {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] | name = Teppōyama Kofun | native_name = 鉄砲山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Teppo-yama Kofun (Gyoda), zenkei.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Teppōyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Teppōyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|7|25.73|N|139|28|41.56|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Gyōda, Saitama]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kantō region]] | type = [[Kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = 6th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes | Historic Site = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Teppōyama Kofun'''|鉄砲山古墳||}} is a ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun]] located in the city of [[Gyōda, Saitama|Gyōda]], [[Saitama Prefecture]], in the [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a National Historic Site in 1938 and re-designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2020 as part of the [[Sakitama Kofun Cluster]].<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/603 |title=埼玉古墳群|trans-title=Saikitama Kofun Cluster|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Teppōyama Kofun has a total length (including moat) of 163 meters, and is thus the third largest tumulus in the Saitama Kofun Cluster. It is a {{nihongo|''[[zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It has the same proportions as the [[Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun]], but shrunk by 80%. [[Archaeological excavation]]s have been conducted in 1967, 1983, 2008 and 2010 to 2011. Artifacts uncovered include cylindrical and "morning glory-shaped" ''[[haniwa]]'', [[Haji pottery]], and [[Sue pottery]] pottery, from which the tumulus is estimated to have been built in the latter half of the 6th century AD.
The ''kofun'' is highly unusual in that it has a triple moat, which is a very rare feature; however, traces of the outermost moat have survived only on the western side of the tumulus, and it is not certain if it once completed encircled the mound. Similar triple-moated ''kofun'' include The Mitsuke Kofun in [[Kurume, Fukuoka]], Tsukioka Kofun in [[Ukiha, Fukuoka]] and the Otsumi Gongenzuka Kofun in [[Sammu, Chiba]].
Another unusual feature is a protrusion extending from the western side of the tumulus, near the joint between the rectangular and circular portions. This is a feature which also appears in the [[Inariyama Kofun]], Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun, and {{ill|Shogunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳|12=draft}} in the Sakitama Kofun Group, but is rarely found in other locations. Called a "Tsukuridashi", it was apparently a platform used for rituals.
From the results of [[ground-penetrating radar]] surveys and excavation on the posterior circular portion of the tumulus, it is known that there is a horizontal-type stone [[burial chamber]] that is orientated to the south. However, no excavation of the burial chamber itself has been conducted, and it is unknown whether or not it has been robbed in antiquity. The entrance to the burial chamber was thickly covered with clay. A Sue pottery pot with a perforated bottom has been excavated from the vestibule.
The name "Teppōyama" comes from the fact that tumulus was used as a gunnery training center of [[Oshi Domain]] during the [[Edo period]]. The training ground was located near the northern constriction, and the mound was modified to form the backstop behind targets used in rifle practice with an embankment constructed to shelter the person near the targets who confirmed whether or not the shooter's aim was correct. A total of 154 bullets in18 varieties have also been found at the site.
;Overall length: 109 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 55 meter diameter x 9.0 meter high ;Anterior rectangular portion: 69 meters wide x 10.1 meters high
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Saitama)]]
==References== {{reflist}}

==Overview== The Teppōyama Kofun has a total length (including moat) of 163 meters, and is thus the third largest tumulus in the Saitama Kofun Cluster. It is a {{nihongo|''[[zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It has the same proportions as the [[Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun]], but shrunk by 80%. [[Archaeological excavation]]s have been conducted in 1967, 1983, 2008 and 2010 to 2011. Artifacts uncovered include cylindrical and "morning glory-shaped" ''[[haniwa]]'', [[Haji pottery]], and [[Sue pottery]] pottery, from which the tumulus is estimated to have been built in the latter half of the 6th century AD.
The ''kofun'' is highly unusual in that it has a triple moat, which is a very rare feature; however, traces of the outermost moat have survived only on the western side of the tumulus, and it is not certain if it once completed encircled the mound. Similar triple-moated ''kofun'' include The Mitsuke Kofun in [[Kurume, Fukuoka]], Tsukioka Kofun in [[Ukiha, Fukuoka]] and the Otsumi Gongenzuka Kofun in [[Sammu, Chiba]].
Another unusual feature is a protrusion extending from the western side of the tumulus, near the joint between the rectangular and circular portions. This is a feature which also appears in the [[Inariyama Kofun]], Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun, and {{ill|Shogunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳|12=draft}} in the Sakitama Kofun Group, but is rarely found in other locations. Called a "Tsukuridashi", it was apparently a platform used for rituals.
From the results of [[ground-penetrating radar]] surveys and excavation on the posterior circular portion of the tumulus, it is known that there is a horizontal-type stone [[burial chamber]] that is orientated to the south. However, no excavation of the burial chamber itself has been conducted, and it is unknown whether or not it has been robbed in antiquity. The entrance to the burial chamber was thickly covered with clay. A Sue pottery pot with a perforated bottom has been excavated from the vestibule.
The name "Teppōyama" comes from the fact that tumulus was used as a gunnery training center of [[Oshi Domain]] during the [[Edo period]]. The training ground was located near the northern constriction, and the mound was modified to form the backstop behind targets used in rifle practice with an embankment constructed to shelter the person near the targets who confirmed whether or not the shooter's aim was correct. A total of 154 bullets in18 varieties have also been found at the site.
;Overall length: 109 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 55 meter diameter x 9.0 meter high ;Anterior rectangular portion: 69 meters wide x 10.1 meters high

Another unusual feature is a protrusion extending from the western side of the tumulus, near the joint between the rectangular and circular portions. This is a feature which also appears in the [[Inariyama Kofun]], Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun, and {{ill|Shogunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳|12=draft}} in the Sakitama Kofun Group, but is rarely found in other locations. Called a "Tsukuridashi", it was apparently a platform used for rituals.
From the results of [[ground-penetrating radar]] surveys and excavation on the posterior circular portion of the tumulus, it is known that there is a horizontal-type stone [[burial chamber]] that is orientated to the south. However, no excavation of the burial chamber itself has been conducted, and it is unknown whether or not it has been robbed in antiquity. The entrance to the burial chamber was thickly covered with clay. A Sue pottery pot with a perforated bottom has been excavated from the vestibule.
The name "Teppōyama" comes from the fact that tumulus was used as a gunnery training center of [[Oshi Domain]] during the [[Edo period]]. The training ground was located near the northern constriction, and the mound was modified to form the backstop behind targets used in rifle practice with an embankment constructed to shelter the person near the targets who confirmed whether or not the shooter's aim was correct. The entrance to the burial chamber was thickly covered with clay. A Sue pottery pot with a perforated bottom has been excavated from the vestibule.
The name "Teppōyama" comes from the fact that tumulus was used as a gunnery training center of [[Oshi Domain]] during the [[Edo period]]. The training ground was located near the northern constriction, and the mound was modified to form the backstop behind targets used in rifle practice with an embankment constructed to shelter the person near the targets who confirmed whether or not the shooter's aim was correct. A total of 154 bullets in18 varieties have also been found at the site.
;Overall length: 109 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 55 meter diameter x 9.0 meter high ;Anterior rectangular portion: 69 meters wide x 10.1 meters high

{{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] | name = Teppōyama Kofun | native_name = 鉄砲山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Teppo-yama Kofun (Gyoda), zenkei.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Teppōyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Teppōyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|7|25.73|N|139|28|41.56|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Gyōda, Saitama]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kantō region]] | type = [[Kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = 6th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes | Historic Site = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Teppōyama Kofun'''|鉄砲山古墳||}} is a ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun]] located in the city of [[Gyōda, Saitama|Gyōda]], [[Saitama Prefecture]], in the [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a National Historic Site in 1938 and re-designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2020 as part of the [[Sakitama Kofun Cluster]].<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/603 |title=埼玉古墳群|trans-title=Saikitama Kofun Cluster|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Teppōyama Kofun has a total length (including moat) of 163 meters, and is thus the third largest tumulus in the Saitama Kofun Cluster. It is a {{nihongo|''[[zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It has the same proportions as the [[Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun]], but shrunk by 80%. [[Archaeological excavation]]s have been conducted in 1967, 1983, 2008 and 2010 to 2011. Artifacts uncovered include cylindrical and "morning glory-shaped" ''[[haniwa]]'', [[Haji pottery]], and [[Sue pottery]] pottery, from which the tumulus is estimated to have been built in the latter half of the 6th century AD.
The ''kofun'' is highly unusual in that it has a triple moat, which is a very rare feature; however, traces of the outermost moat have survived only on the western side of the tumulus, and it is not certain if it once completed encircled the mound. Similar triple-moated ''kofun'' include The Mitsuke Kofun in [[Kurume, Fukuoka]], Tsukioka Kofun in [[Ukiha, Fukuoka]] and the Otsumi Gongenzuka Kofun in [[Sammu, Chiba]].
Another unusual feature is a protrusion extending from the western side of the tumulus, near the joint between the rectangular and circular portions. This is a feature which also appears in the [[Inariyama Kofun]], Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun, and {{ill|Shogunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳|12=draft}} in the Sakitama Kofun Group, but is rarely found in other locations. Called a "Tsukuridashi", it was apparently a platform used for rituals.
From the results of [[ground-penetrating radar]] surveys and excavation on the posterior circular portion of the tumulus, it is known that there is a horizontal-type stone [[burial chamber]] that is orientated to the south. However, no excavation of the burial chamber itself has been conducted, and it is unknown whether or not it has been robbed in antiquity. The entrance to the burial chamber was thickly covered with clay. A Sue pottery pot with a perforated bottom has been excavated from the vestibule.
The name "Teppōyama" comes from the fact that tumulus was used as a gunnery training center of [[Oshi Domain]] during the [[Edo period]]. The training ground was located near the northern constriction, and the mound was modified to form the backstop behind targets used in rifle practice with an embankment constructed to shelter the person near the targets who confirmed whether or not the shooter's aim was correct. A total of 154 bullets in18 varieties have also been found at the site.
;Overall length: 109 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 55 meter diameter x 9.0 meter high ;Anterior rectangular portion: 69 meters wide x 10.1 meters high

revision.text:
{{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] | name = Teppōyama Kofun | native_name = 鉄砲山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Teppo-yama Kofun (Gyoda), zenkei.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Teppōyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Teppōyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|7|25.73|N|139|28|41.56|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Gyōda, Saitama]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kantō region]] | type = [[Kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = 6th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Teppōyama Kofun'''|鉄砲山古墳||}} is a ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun]] located in the city of [[Gyōda, Saitama|Gyōda]], [[Saitama Prefecture]], in the [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a National Historic Site in 1938 and re-designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2020 as part of the [[Sakitama Kofun Cluster]].<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/603 |title=埼玉古墳群|trans-title=Saikitama Kofun Cluster|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Teppōyama Kofun has a total length (including moat) of 163 meters, and is thus the third largest tumulus in the Saitama Kofun Cluster. It is a {{nihongo|''[[zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It has the same proportions as the [[Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun]], but shrunk by 80%. [[Archaeological excavation]]s have been conducted in 1967, 1983, 2008 and 2010 to 2011. Artifacts uncovered include cylindrical and "morning glory-shaped" ''[[haniwa]]'', [[Haji pottery]], and [[Sue pottery]] pottery, from which the tumulus is estimated to have been built in the latter half of the 6th century AD.
The ''kofun'' is highly unusual in that it has a triple moat, which is a very rare feature; however, traces of the outermost moat have survived only on the western side of the tumulus, and it is not certain if it once completed encircled the mound. Similar triple-moated ''kofun'' include The Mitsuke Kofun in [[Kurume, Fukuoka]], Tsukioka Kofun in [[Ukiha, Fukuoka]] and the Otsumi Gongenzuka Kofun in [[Sammu, Chiba]].
Another unusual feature is a protrusion extending from the western side of the tumulus, near the joint between the rectangular and circular portions. This is a feature which also appears in the [[Inariyama Kofun]], Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun, and {{ill|Shogunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shogunyama Kofun|qq=}} in the Sakitama Kofun Group, but is rarely found in other locations. Called a "Tsukuridashi", it was apparently a platform used for rituals.
From the results of [[ground-penetrating radar]] surveys and excavation on the posterior circular portion of the tumulus, it is known that there is a horizontal-type stone [[burial chamber]] that is orientated to the south. However, no excavation of the burial chamber itself has been conducted, and it is unknown whether or not it has been robbed in antiquity. The entrance to the burial chamber was thickly covered with clay. A Sue pottery pot with a perforated bottom has been excavated from the vestibule.
The name "Teppōyama" comes from the fact that tumulus was used as a gunnery training center of [[Oshi Domain]] during the [[Edo period]]. The training ground was located near the northern constriction, and the mound was modified to form the backstop behind targets used in rifle practice with an embankment constructed to shelter the person near the targets who confirmed whether or not the shooter's aim was correct. A total of 154 bullets in18 varieties have also been found at the site.
;Overall length: 109 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 55 meter diameter x 9.0 meter high ;Anterior rectangular portion: 69 meters wide x 10.1 meters high
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Saitama)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons Category:kk]][[Category:-inline|Teppoyama Kofun}} *[https://www.city.gyoda.lg.jp/15/04/12/map/tetu.html Gyōda city home page] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://sakitama-muse.spec.ed.jp/埼玉古墳群紹介/鉄砲山古墳 Museum of the Sakitama Ancient Kofuns] {{in lang|ja}}
{{Kofun navbar}}

Called a "Tsukuridashi", it was apparently a platform used for rituals.
From the results of [[ground-penetrating radar]] surveys and excavation on the posterior circular portion of the tumulus, it is known that there is a horizontal-type stone [[burial chamber]] that is orientated to the south. However, no excavation of the burial chamber itself has been conducted, and it is unknown whether or not it has been robbed in antiquity. The entrance to the burial chamber was thickly covered with clay. A Sue pottery pot with a perforated bottom has been excavated from the vestibule.
The name "Teppōyama" comes from the fact that tumulus was used as a gunnery training center of [[Oshi Domain]] during the [[Edo period]]. The training ground was located near the northern constriction, and the mound was modified to form the backstop behind targets used in rifle practice with an embankment constructed to shelter the person near the targets who confirmed whether or not the shooter's aim was correct. A total of 154 bullets in18 varieties have also been found at the site.
;Overall length: 109 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 55 meter diameter x 9.0 meter high ;Anterior rectangular portion: 69 meters wide x 10.1 meters high

{{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] | name = Teppōyama Kofun | native_name = 鉄砲山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Teppo-yama Kofun (Gyoda), zenkei.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Teppōyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Teppōyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|7|25.73|N|139|28|41.56|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Gyōda, Saitama]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kantō region]] | type = [[Kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = 6th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Teppōyama Kofun'''|鉄砲山古墳||}} is a ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun]] located in the city of [[Gyōda, Saitama|Gyōda]], [[Saitama Prefecture]], in the [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a National Historic Site in 1938 and re-designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2020 as part of the [[Sakitama Kofun Cluster]].<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/603 |title=埼玉古墳群|trans-title=Saikitama Kofun Cluster|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Teppōyama Kofun has a total length (including moat) of 163 meters, and is thus the third largest tumulus in the Saitama Kofun Cluster. It is a {{nihongo|''[[zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It has the same proportions as the [[Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun]], but shrunk by 80%. [[Archaeological excavation]]s have been conducted in 1967, 1983, 2008 and 2010 to 2011. Artifacts uncovered include cylindrical and "morning glory-shaped" ''[[haniwa]]'', [[Haji pottery]], and [[Sue pottery]] pottery, from which the tumulus is estimated to have been built in the latter half of the 6th century AD.
The ''kofun'' is highly unusual in that it has a triple moat, which is a very rare feature; however, traces of the outermost moat have survived only on the western side of the tumulus, and it is not certain if it once completed encircled the mound. Similar triple-moated ''kofun'' include The Mitsuke Kofun in [[Kurume, Fukuoka]], Tsukioka Kofun in [[Ukiha, Fukuoka]] and the Otsumi Gongenzuka Kofun in [[Sammu, Chiba]].
Another unusual feature is a protrusion extending from the western side of the tumulus, near the joint between the rectangular and circular portions. This is a feature which also appears in the [[Inariyama Kofun]], Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun, and {{ill|Shogunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shogunyama Kofun|qq=}} in the Sakitama Kofun Group, but is rarely found in other locations. Called a "Tsukuridashi", it was apparently a platform used for rituals.
From the results of [[ground-penetrating radar]] surveys and excavation on the posterior circular portion of the tumulus, it is known that there is a horizontal-type stone [[burial chamber]] that is orientated to the south. However, no excavation of the burial chamber itself has been conducted, and it is unknown whether or not it has been robbed in antiquity. The entrance to the burial chamber was thickly covered with clay. A Sue pottery pot with a perforated bottom has been excavated from the vestibule.
The name "Teppōyama" comes from the fact that tumulus was used as a gunnery training center of [[Oshi Domain]] during the [[Edo period]]. The training ground was located near the northern constriction, and the mound was modified to form the backstop behind targets used in rifle practice with an embankment constructed to shelter the person near the targets who confirmed whether or not the shooter's aim was correct. A total of 154 bullets in18 varieties have also been found at the site.
;Overall length: 109 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 55 meter diameter x 9.0 meter high ;Anterior rectangular portion: 69 meters wide x 10.1 meters high

{{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] | name = Teppōyama Kofun | native_name = 鉄砲山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Teppo-yama Kofun (Gyoda), zenkei.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Teppōyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Teppōyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|7|25.73|N|139|28|41.56|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Gyōda, Saitama]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kantō region]] | type = [[Kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = 6th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Teppōyama Kofun'''|鉄砲山古墳||}} is a ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun]] located in the city of [[Gyōda, Saitama|Gyōda]], [[Saitama Prefecture]], in the [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a National Historic Site in 1938 and re-designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2020 as part of the [[Sakitama Kofun Cluster]].<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/603 |title=埼玉古墳群|trans-title=Saikitama Kofun Cluster|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Teppōyama Kofun has a total length (including moat) of 163 meters, and is thus the third largest tumulus in the Saitama Kofun Cluster. It is a {{nihongo|''[[zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above. It has the same proportions as the [[Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun]], but shrunk by 80%. [[Archaeological excavation]]s have been conducted in 1967, 1983, 2008 and 2010 to 2011. Artifacts uncovered include cylindrical and "morning glory-shaped" ''[[haniwa]]'', [[Haji pottery]], and [[Sue pottery]] pottery, from which the tumulus is estimated to have been built in the latter half of the 6th century AD.
The ''kofun'' is highly unusual in that it has a triple moat, which is a very rare feature; however, traces of the outermost moat have survived only on the western side of the tumulus, and it is not certain if it once completed encircled the mound. Similar triple-moated ''kofun'' include The Mitsuke Kofun in [[Kurume, Fukuoka]], Tsukioka Kofun in [[Ukiha, Fukuoka]] and the Otsumi Gongenzuka Kofun in [[Sammu, Chiba]].
Another unusual feature is a protrusion extending from the western side of the tumulus, near the joint between the rectangular and circular portions. This is a feature which also appears in the [[Inariyama Kofun]], Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun, and {{ill|Shogunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shogunyama Kofun|qq=}} in the Sakitama Kofun Group, but is rarely found in other locations. Called a "Tsukuridashi", it was apparently a platform used for rituals.
From the results of [[ground-penetrating radar]] surveys and excavation on the posterior circular portion of the tumulus, it is known that there is a horizontal-type stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] that is orientated to the south. However, no excavation of the burial chamber itself has been conducted, and it is unknown whether or not it has been robbed in antiquity. The entrance to the burial chamber was thickly covered with clay. A Sue pottery pot with a perforated bottom has been excavated from the vestibule.
The name "Teppōyama" comes from the fact that tumulus was used as a gunnery training center of [[Oshi Domain]] during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. The training ground was located near the northern constriction, and the mound was modified to form the backstop behind targets used in rifle practice with an embankment constructed to shelter the person near the targets who confirmed whether or not the shooter's aim was correct. A total of 154 bullets in18 varieties have also been found at the site.
;Overall length: 109 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 55 meter diameter x 9.0 meter high ;Anterior rectangular portion: 69 meters wide x 10.1 meters high
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Saitama)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons Category:kk]][[Category:-inline|Teppoyama Kofun}} *[https://www.city.gyoda.lg.jp/15/04/12/map/tetu.html Gyōda city home page] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://sakitama-muse.spec.ed.jp/埼玉古墳群紹介/鉄砲山古墳 Museum of the Sakitama Ancient Kofuns] {{in lang|ja}}

The ''kofun'' is highly unusual in that it has a triple moat, which is a very rare feature; however, traces of the outermost moat have survived only on the western side of the tumulus, and it is not certain if it once completed encircled the mound. Similar triple-moated ''kofun'' include The Mitsuke Kofun in [[Kurume, Fukuoka]], Tsukioka Kofun in [[Ukiha, Fukuoka]] and the Otsumi Gongenzuka Kofun in [[Sammu, Chiba]].
Another unusual feature is a protrusion extending from the western side of the tumulus, near the joint between the rectangular and circular portions. This is a feature which also appears in the [[Inariyama Kofun]], Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun, and {{ill|Shogunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shogunyama Kofun|qq=}} in the Sakitama Kofun Group, but is rarely found in other locations. Called a "Tsukuridashi", it was apparently a platform used for rituals.
From the results of [[:en:Ground-penetrating radar|Ground-penetrating radar]] surveys and excavation on the posterior circular portion of the tumulus, it is known that there is a horizontal-type stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] that is orientated to the south. However, no excavation of the burial chamber itself has been conducted, and it is unknown whether or not it has been robbed in antiquity. The entrance to the burial chamber was thickly covered with clay. A Sue pottery pot with a perforated bottom has been excavated from the vestibule.
The name "Teppōyama" comes from the fact that tumulus was used as a gunnery training center of [[Oshi Domain]] during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. The training ground was located near the northern constriction, and the mound was modified to form the backstop behind targets used in rifle practice with an embankment constructed to shelter the person near the targets who confirmed whether or not the shooter's aim was correct. A total of 154 bullets in18 varieties have also been found at the site.
